Which do you think is the best model? So you sell the product first with a forced continuity newsletter? Well, there is no worst model or best model. There may be for a particular individual in a particular set of circumstances. Ideally, in a perfect world, you would have, as I described to Harvey yesterday, you would really have three businesses stacked on top of each other. I'll be lazy and won't go around, I'll draw it. Maybe I'll draw, oh yeah, here we go. God forbid they should take the pens out of the box. I guess another $5,000 a day. Think this, Dave. So this one is sort of a traditional information marketing business. So we got product with forced continuity at the bottom, then we have a higher level of continuity, then we have some kind of coaching, and maybe we have another higher level of coaching. In here is one-on-one stuff. We do it for you stuff. And then way up here at the top is, so somebody's going to give you $200,000 to completely come and fix their mess or create their business from scratch. Now you could run any one of these without the other two. You could run this, you could run this, you could run this, you could run this and this, you could run this and this. You could start here, actually go backwards. Under the under utopian circumstances, somebody does all three. But because if you have this, you have no pipeline. What this does is give you a pipeline where customers have a place to mature, right? Whereas when you bring them directly in at the middle or the high end, you're always bringing them in directly at the middle or the high end. So if you have the pipeline full of maturing customers who over a year or two years or three years or four years or five years are getting ready, at some point in time you no longer have to do anything in order to fill the top two. They're coming up out of the pipeline begging to get there. However, creating the bottom pyramid, that's like a real business. I mean, it's got ask bill. I mean, it's got infrastructure and it's got employees and it's got products and it's got ads in 20 magazines and six step sequences. And I'm not telling you anything. You don't know. So for some people, I advise skipping it. For some people I advise go back and build it after you got that other thing. For some people I say you got to have it. So go do that first and worry about the other later. So like in Charlie's case, I mean, we had long conversations about this with Charlie. Should he have that bottom? And you've been privy to those conversations. And the answer is yes, he should, but as a practical matter, he has no time to even pay attention to it. And his chair side value in his practice is so high that it's hard to take him away from the practice. Plus, like me, he has a longer list of things he should say no to than he says no to. So, you know, he's dental practice, spa, car wash, real estate, I mean, you know, so there's no time. But the good news is there are resources. So we could skip all that spend $9,000 to make the sale. Now, in three years, he still has to go spend $9,000 to go get the next new guy to fill the hole that just occurred if we don't go back and do this. Right? So it's almost almost which model is not the right question has to which model for which person at which time in which order is really the right question.
